**Celery root**, also called **celeriac**, **turnip-rooted celery**, and **knob celery**, is a variety of celery grown for its large root.

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

The roots are large and generally rounded in shape, with a bumpy tan/brown exterior.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) The inner flesh is white and crisp,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) like that of parsnip, rutabaga, and kohlrabi.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) Flavor-wise, they are overall milder than celery stalk but still have an aromatic flavor like it and parsley.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)

## Seasonality

[edit | edit source]

Celery roots are available year-round with a peak in the fall and winter.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

When picking celery root, look for firm, dense, non-dehydrated bulbs.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) You'll want to avoid excessively large bulbs, which tend to be spongy in texture. Smaller bulbs have a slightly more pronounced flavor, which may be important if you're planning to cook and serve the vegetable on its own.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) If you do buy the roots with the tops, trim the stalks and save them for soups and stews.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Like other root vegetables, celery root can be kept in a cool, dark, dry place like a root cellar for a few weeks.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) The refrigerator is also a fine choice.[[6]](#cite_note-:3-6)

## Preparation

[edit | edit source]

To prepare celery roots for cooking, start by scrubbing them to remove the dirt. Trim off the top and bottom to remove any remaining stalk and root end portions.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) Peeling") can be difficult, especially if the skin is very rough—a sharp knife may be a better choice for removing the exterior than a peeler.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) After cutting it into pieces as desired, you can keep the celery root in acidulated water to prevent browning.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) Be sure to discard any spongy parts.

## Use

[edit | edit source]

The celery root is routinely used in French cooking and is seen throughout Europe, especially in the north of the region.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) It is often cooked and mashed or used in casseroles and baked dishes. It can also be eaten raw in salads for a mild crunchy addition.[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)[[6]](#cite_note-:3-6) Like celery stalk, the root can be used as an aromatic to flavor soups.[[7]](#cite_note-7) As an autumnal food, it pairs well with similarly hearty seasonal ingredients, such as apple, mushroom, brassicas, game, pork, and duck.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) Avoid overcooking celery root, as it will go quickly from firm to mushy.
